frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: Hi Neha, just wanted to check in after that incident near Manyata Tech Park. Look, I know it's Bengaluru and we often work late, but please be extra careful. Always try to leave before 8 PM if you can, especially if you're alone. If not, make sure you take an office cab or travel in a group. Apna khayal rakho, the city is just not safe these days, unfortunately.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment, coming from an HR manager, frames safety as the woman's personal responsibility, advising her to restrict her timings and travel in groups. It disguises victim-blaming as practical advice and concern ('apna khayal rakho') by referencing a general 'unsafe city' rather than male predatory behavior. This aligns with 'women advising women' and 'just being practical' from the frame.
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 32. Married. A woman working as an HR manager in Bengaluru. You are dark skinned. You have been dark skinned your whole life and you know exactly what that has meant in every room you have entered. You are on a panel at a women in leadership conference. The moderator has asked the panellists what barriers they faced on their way up. You are giving your answer. Your answer is honest and specific and names colourism directly. But in the middle of your answer you say something that reveals you have also internalised part of the standard you are critiquing Ñ you mention that you learned to compensate, that you dressed better, that you made sure your work was twice as visible. You frame this as strategy. It is also capitulation.
